recent_summary: Cocoa prices have been volatile over the past week, falling to over 1-month lows before rallying into the weekend. Prices retreated mid-week on robust Ivory Coast cocoa exports and forecasts for beneficial West African weather, with abundant rains reportedly boosting the mid-crop outlook. However, prices found support from intermittent concerns about crop-damaging weather and tight inventories, with Wells Fargo noting inventory constraints continue to underpin the market.
background_context: West Africa produces roughly two-thirds of global cocoa and experienced up to 40% harvest losses across two recent seasons, creating a structural supply deficit. Major chocolate manufacturers like Barry Callebaut are adjusting strategies amid cocoa volatility, with the company forecasting profit declines following sharp cocoa price drops. Industry players are increasingly focused on premiumisation strategies (Mars, Mondelēz) to offset higher input costs, while demand concerns persist as consumers face elevated chocolate prices. Ghana's COCOBOD has maintained producer prices at GH¢2,587 per bag for the 2025/2026 light crop season.
key_themes: West African weather patterns driving short-term price volatility
Demand destruction concerns amid elevated chocolate prices
Structural supply constraints from recent harvest losses maintaining price floor
signal_rationale: Improved weather forecasts and rising Ivory Coast exports are weighing on prices, but tight global inventories and structural supply deficits from prior season losses provide underlying support, creating a tug-of-war between near-term bearish and longer-term bullish factors.

watch_items: Ivory Coast and Ghana mid-crop harvest progress and export data
West African rainfall patterns over next 2-4 weeks
European and North American grinding data for demand signals
data_quality: Coverage is adequate with good representation from market data sources (Barchart, TradingView) and industry outlets, though direct Reuters/Bloomberg cocoa-specific articles are limited in the recent timeframe.

demand_forecast.full_year_outlook: Analysts expect cocoa prices to decline in 2026 from 2024-2025 highs as the market transitions from deficit to projected surplus conditions, with London cocoa forecasted to average around £3,400/tonne (~$4,586 USD).
demand_forecast.price_targets: ING forecasts London cocoa averaging approximately £3,400/tonne (~$4,586 USD) for 2026
demand_forecast.key_risks_upside: Supply disruptions from adverse weather and disease in West Africa persisting beyond expectations
demand_forecast.key_risks_downside: Weaker processing demand with global grindings estimated to fall to 4.60 million tonnes in 2024/25
demand_forecast.source_summary: Capital.com analysis citing ING commodities research and ICCA November 2025 bulletin; TradingEconomics provided no substantive content
